M114.use: When early growth looks flat and you can't tell if it's failing
M114.body:
Apply **Flywheel / Autocatalysis** to my situation.

A self-reinforcing loop where each turn makes the next turn easier — lower prices bring customers, bring sellers, lower costs, lower prices. It is slow to start and nearly unstoppable once spinning, which is why the hard question is whether an unimpressive early curve is failure or just the first revolutions.

Do this:
1. Draw the loop as labeled arrows: A → B → C → back to A. Each arrow must be a causal claim in the form "more X CAUSES more Y." The loop must close. If it doesn't close, you have a value chain, not a flywheel — say so and stop.
2. Audit every arrow. For each, cite the evidence it fires, its magnitude, and its lag in weeks or months. Mark any arrow with no evidence as ASSUMED.
3. Find the weakest link — the lowest-gain or slowest arrow. That single link sets the speed of the whole loop; nothing else matters until it moves.
4. Count the turns completed so far, and compute the loop's gain: after one full turn, is the input to the next turn actually larger? Give the multiplier.

Then: tell me whether my flat curve is a loop starting or a loop that isn't closed, and which single arrow to push.

Rules:
- Do not draw a wish-loop. Boxes connected by arrows because they belong on the same slide is the standard fake here. Every arrow needs a mechanism and evidence, or it is marked ASSUMED and the loop is unproven.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M115.use: When the strategy promises premium quality at the lowest price
M115.body:
Apply **Generic Strategies (Cost Leadership vs. Differentiation)** to my situation.

A business must be either the low-cost producer or meaningfully differentiated. Trying to be both leaves it stuck in the middle with neither advantage — the low-cost player undercuts it and the differentiated player out-values it. The model's job is to force a choice that has been avoided.

Do this:
1. Make me declare: COST or DIFFERENTIATION. One word, before any analysis.
2. Now check the declaration against reality. If COST: rank my unit cost against the three nearest competitors and state whether I am genuinely lowest, and by what structural reason (scale, process, input access) — not by underpaying people or deferring maintenance. If DIFFERENTIATION: name the premium I charge, in percent, and who pays it and why.
3. Hunt for the straddle: quote the places in my strategy, pitch, or roadmap where I promise both. List them verbatim. Each one is a decision not made.
4. For each straddle, state the cost of resolving it — what customer, feature, or channel I must give up to commit.

Then: tell me which position I actually occupy today, whether it's a position at all, and the one thing to abandon this quarter to commit.

Rules:
- "We're differentiated AND efficient" is the sound of the middle. Do not accept it. If both are claimed, the answer is stuck-in-the-middle until proven otherwise with numbers.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M116.use: The cleanest single test of whether a moat exists
M116.body:
Apply **Pricing Power** to my situation.

The ability to raise price without losing meaningful volume is the cleanest observable test that a moat exists — a business with no advantage cannot survive a price increase. This model is diagnostic, and it runs on evidence from actual price changes, nothing else.

Do this:
1. Ask the only question: when did I last raise price, by how much, and what happened to volume in the following two quarters? Give the numbers.
2. If the answer is "never" or "we don't know": do not infer power from margins or brand. Mark it UNRESOLVED and go to step 3.
3. Design the test. Pick one segment, geography, or SKU where a real increase can be run. State the increase (start at 5-10%), the duration, the volume loss that would still leave me better off, and the number that ends the test.
4. Interpret the last increase or the coming one: was volume loss below the break-even churn? Say what that implies about who my customers think my substitutes are.

Then: tell me whether I have pricing power, and the exact price test to run next quarter with its decision rule.

Rules:
- Fat margins, a strong brand, and loyal customers are not pricing power — they are the story people tell instead of raising the price. Only an executed price increase and the observed volume response counts as evidence. Everything else is UNRESOLVED.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M117.use: When the advantage survives publishing the playbook
M117.body:
Apply **Process Power** to my situation.

Embedded organizational routines that deliver superior cost or quality and cannot be copied even when fully disclosed — because copying requires years of hysteretic change through a whole organization. Toyota's rivals toured the plants, read the books, and still lost. The test is publication.

Do this:
1. Name the routine as behavior: what people do, daily, that produces the result. Not a department, not a tool, not a value.
2. Run the publication test. Hand a competitor a complete manual of this routine plus my best three people. Say precisely why they still fail — and if they wouldn't fail, this is not process power. Say so.
3. If it survives: quantify the hysteresis. How many years of continuous change, at what cost, with what rate of executive turnover, would a rival need? Why can't they buy the years?
4. Separate what's actually load-bearing from what's ceremony: list which parts of the routine could be dropped without changing the output.

Then: tell me whether my advantage is a routine or a purchase, and what it would take to destroy it from the inside.

Rules:
- Being good at operations is not process power. If it lives in software I bought, in people I hired, or in a founder's head, a rival can buy, hire, or poach it — that is competence, not power. Only routines that survive full disclosure qualify. Most don't.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M118.use: When growth is being offered as evidence of health
M118.body:
Apply **Unit Economics** to my situation.

One unit — one customer, one store, one order — must earn more over its life than it cost to acquire and serve. Scaling a negative-margin unit does not fix it; it multiplies it. This model exists to stop growth from being used as proof of health.

Do this:
1. Define the unit and state the cohort you're measuring. One unit, one cohort, one time window. Not blended, not company-wide.
2. Build contribution margin bottom-up: revenue per unit minus every variable cost to serve it — COGS, payments, support, hosting, returns, the sales commission, the refunds. Not gross margin. Not revenue.
3. Compute CAC fully loaded — all sales and marketing spend divided by units acquired in that period, including the salaries. Then payback in months, and LTV using OBSERVED cohort retention, not an assumed churn rate.
4. Ask the scale question directly: does contribution margin per unit rise, stay flat, or fall as volume grows? Name the specific cost that would have to fall, by how much, for the unit to work.

Then: tell me whether to grow, fix, or stop — and if fix, the one line item that has to move.

Rules:
- Do not let blended numbers, gross margin, or "it improves at scale" past. Those three are how broken unit economics get reported as healthy. If a cost falls at scale, name it, name the driver, and show the volume required.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M119.use: To find where the profit pools and whether it's your step
M119.body:
Apply **Value Chain** to my situation.

Decompose the industry into the sequence of activities from raw input to end customer, then ask where the profit actually pools and why. It is rarely spread evenly, and it is rarely where the work is. Use it to locate the choke point worth owning — and to notice when your own step is the one being commoditized.

Do this:
1. List the steps of the INDUSTRY chain, raw input to end customer. Every step, including the ones nobody talks about. Name the firms occupying each.
2. Estimate margin and return on capital at each step. Rough numbers, labeled as estimates, are fine — the ranking matters more than precision.
3. For the top two steps, explain WHY profit pools there: scarcity, scale, standards control, ownership of the customer, regulation. If you can't name the reason, the pool is temporary.
4. Mark my step. Say who upstream and downstream can squeeze me, and whether my step is being commoditized — cite the evidence.

Then: tell me the choke point in this chain, whether I can reach it from where I stand, and what the move costs.

Rules:
- Map the INDUSTRY's chain, not my company's org chart. Listing my internal departments and their costs is the standard substitution — it produces a cost audit, not a profit map. The chain crosses company boundaries or you've done the wrong exercise.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M120.use: Before buying your supplier or your channel
M120.body:
Apply **Vertical Integration** to my situation.

Owning your suppliers or distribution instead of transacting with them is worth doing when the market for that input genuinely fails — thin markets, hold-up risk, asset specificity, or when controlling the customer experience IS the product. The cost is capital intensity and lost flexibility. Integrate where the transaction fails, not to capture someone else's margin.

Do this:
1. Name the exact step under consideration and the counterparty I'd be replacing.
2. Run the market-failure test. Score each: how many credible suppliers exist (thin market?); can they hold me up once I've invested (asset specificity?); does contracting require specifying things I can't specify in advance; is this step where the customer's experience is made? If none score, the market works — and the answer is don't integrate.
3. Price the ownership honestly: capital required, the fixed cost that survives a downturn, the flexibility surrendered, and my expected efficiency versus a specialist who does only this all day.
4. Compare against the cheaper alternatives I skipped: a long-term contract, an equity stake, a second source, exclusivity.

Then: tell me integrate or contract, and if integrate, the specific market failure that justifies it.

Rules:
- "Capture their margin" is not a reason. Their margin is compensation for capital and risk I would be absorbing, and their profit becomes my cost line. If margin capture is the whole case, the answer is no.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M121.use: When a system behaves in a way nobody can explain
M121.body:
Apply **Abstraction Layers** to my situation.

Complex systems are made tractable by stacking levels, each hiding the mechanics of the level below and exposing only a simplified interface. All abstractions leak — when behavior turns inexplicable, the answer is in the level that was hidden.

Do this:
1. Name the layer stack in my situation, from the surface I actually touch down to the physical or human substrate. Four to six levels, each hiding the one beneath it.
2. Say which layer I am currently reasoning at, and whether my problem is stated at the wrong altitude.
3. Point at the leak: name the specific symptom where a lower layer's mechanics are showing through the interface I was promised.
4. Tell me the one level to drop to, and exactly what to inspect there.

Then: tell me the altitude to work at, and the single leak worth opening the hood for.

Rules:
- Do not just call my situation complex and list its parts. Layers are ordered by what hides what. A list with no hiding relation is an inventory, not a stack — that is how this model gets faked.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M122.use: When you keep re-deciding the same thing from scratch
M122.body:
Apply **Algorithms** to my situation.

An algorithm is a finite, unambiguous procedure that transforms inputs into outputs the same way on every run, so the result depends on the process rather than on the judgment or mood of whoever executes it.

Do this:
1. Identify the decision in my situation that recurs — the one I keep re-deriving. If nothing recurs, say so and stop.
2. Write the procedure as numbered steps, each with a named input and a named output. Every step that would need judgment must be replaced by a threshold, a count, or a named tiebreaker.
3. State the termination condition and the inputs the procedure must refuse.
4. Trace my current situation through it, step by step, and show me the output it produces.

Then: hand me a procedure someone else could run to get my answer without me in the room.

Rules:
- A list of vague prompts is not an algorithm. If any step could yield two different answers from identical inputs, it is underspecified — fix it or say plainly that this decision cannot be encoded.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M123.use: Before a moment when your judgment will be compromised
M123.body:
Apply **Conditional Logic (If-Statements)** to my situation.

Behavior written as explicit condition-action branches — if this observable state holds, take that action — removes discretion at the moment emotion is highest, and exposes the branches nobody specified.

Do this:
1. Name the future moment in my situation when my judgment will be worst — the panic, the pressure, the temptation. Be specific about when it arrives.
2. Write the policy as IF/THEN lines. Each condition must be observable by someone who is not me and cannot be argued about: a number, a date, a countable event. No "if things get bad."
3. Write the ELSE: the states my rules do not cover. List at least three unhandled branches I never specified.
4. For each rule, name who or what enforces it when I want to override it.

Then: give me the rules I commit to now, in writing, before the moment arrives.

Rules:
- Conditions that require my in-the-moment judgment defeat the entire model — the point is removing discretion, not relocating it. Reject any trigger I could rationalize away.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M124.use: When the same problem shows up at every level
M124.body:
Apply **Recursion** to my situation.

Recursion solves a problem by applying the same procedure to smaller instances of itself until a base case halts it. Self-similar structures — ownership chains, org layers, nested arguments, effects of effects — yield to it. Recursion without a base case never terminates, which is what runaway feedback and infinite regress look like in the wild.

Do this:
1. State whether my situation is actually self-similar: name the structure that repeats and show it repeating at two different scales.
2. Write the procedure that applies at one level, then apply it literally to the next level down. Do this three levels deep and show the work at each.
3. Name the base case — the condition where the descent stops. State it as a testable condition.
4. If you cannot find a base case, say so loudly and name what runs away: which quantity grows without bound and how fast.

Then: tell me either the depth where this bottoms out, or that it doesn't — and what that means for me.

Rules:
- Repeating a word at different sizes is not recursion. The same procedure must genuinely apply to the smaller instance, or drop the model.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M125.use: Before adding people, money, or machines to speed something up
M125.body:
Apply **Amdahl's Law and Bottlenecks** to my situation.

Total speedup is capped by the fraction of work that cannot be parallelized or accelerated. Optimizing anything other than the constraint yields nearly nothing, no matter how much capacity you add.

Do this:
1. Break my process into its actual stages with a rough time or cost for each. Say what fraction of total elapsed time each stage owns.
2. Mark each stage parallelizable or strictly serial. The serial fraction is the one that must wait for the one before it.
3. Compute the ceiling: if I made every parallelizable stage instant and free, how much faster does the whole thing finish? Give me the number.
4. Name the single constraint, and propose fixes that act only on it. Explicitly list what I am currently optimizing that is not the constraint.

Then: tell me the maximum improvement available, and the one place to spend to get it.

Rules:
- The seductive error is optimizing the visible, tractable, fun stage instead of the bottleneck. If your recommendation would leave the serial fraction untouched, you have wasted my money — say so instead.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M126.use: When deciding whether to check everything or use a rule of thumb
M126.body:
Apply **Brute Force vs. Heuristics** to my situation.

Brute force enumerates every possibility and is guaranteed correct but often unaffordable. A heuristic is a cheap rule that is usually right and occasionally badly wrong. The skill is knowing which regime you are in.

Do this:
1. Count the option space. Give me an actual number or order of magnitude for how many possibilities exist. If you must estimate, label it an estimate.
2. Price one full enumeration: hours, dollars, attention. Then price the cost of being wrong once.
3. Declare the regime. Small space plus high stakes means enumerate — then start enumerating, right here. Exploding space means heuristic.
4. If heuristic: state the rule in one line, and name the specific case where it fails badly and how I would notice.

Then: tell me which regime I am in, and either the enumeration or the rule plus its blind spot.

Rules:
- Do not hedge into "use both." The model's whole value is committing to a regime after counting. And a heuristic offered without its failure case is not a heuristic, it is a guess wearing a badge.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M127.use: When you're reusing an answer you worked out a while ago
M127.body:
Apply **Caching and Memoization** to my situation.

Caching stores the result of expensive work so the next request is answered from memory instead of recomputed — trading space for time. It explains why expertise, checklists, and institutional memory pay. The hard part is always invalidation: a cache quietly serving stale answers is worse than no cache.

Do this:
1. List what I am currently answering from cache rather than recomputing: beliefs, rules of thumb, saved decisions, "we always do it this way." Name at least four.
2. For each, date it. When was this actually computed, and what were the inputs then?
3. For each, write the invalidation condition: the specific event that would make this answer wrong. If no event could, it is not knowledge, it is dogma.
4. Name which entries are stale right now, and which expensive recomputation is worth caching going forward.

Then: tell me the one stale cached answer I am still acting on.

Rules:
- The failure here is never the storing, it is the not-invalidating. Do not give me a filing system. Find the stale entry.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M128.use: When a plan claims all the interactions were considered
M128.body:
Apply **Combinatorial Explosion** to my situation.

Combinations grow exponentially with the number of elements, so systems that look modestly complicated have state spaces nobody can enumerate or test. Any sufficiently configurable system is running in a state it was never tested in.

Do this:
1. Count the moving parts: every option, toggle, role, exception, edge case, or party whose state can vary independently. List them.
2. Multiply. Give me the actual size of the state space, and how it grows when I add one more part.
3. Estimate how many of those states have ever actually been exercised or tested. The ratio is the point — state it.
4. Name the parts whose removal collapses the space fastest, and the untested combination most likely to be occupied right now.

Then: tell me how much of my system has never been tried, and which single part to delete.

Rules:
- The misuse is treating this as an excuse for fatalism, or listing parts without multiplying. Do the arithmetic and name a part to remove — the model's payload is fewer moving parts, not despair.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M129.use: To test whether you actually understand something
M129.body:
Apply **Compression and Kolmogorov Complexity** to my situation.

The shortest description that reproduces a thing measures how much structure it has: regularities compress, noise does not. If you can restate a body of knowledge far more briefly without losing its predictions, you have found the theory.

Do this:
1. Restate my situation in 40 words. Then in 12. Then in 5.
2. At each level, list the predictions that survive — the concrete things the compressed version still tells me will happen.
3. Name what fell out under compression, and sort it into two piles: detail that was noise, and detail that was load-bearing and proves the compression failed.
4. Give me the shortest version that keeps every load-bearing prediction. That is the theory of my situation.

Then: tell me whether my situation has a structure I understand, or whether I have been memorizing noise.

Rules:
- Fewer words is not compression. A summary that drops predictions is lossy and worthless here. The test is that the short version must still generate the same forecasts as the long one — if it can't, say the situation doesn't compress and tell me what that means.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M130.use: When hunting a cause and you don't know where to start
M130.body:
Apply **Divide and Conquer** to my situation.

Repeatedly split a problem in half and discard the half that cannot contain the answer, turning a linear slog into a logarithmic one. Always ask which single question eliminates the most possibilities.

Do this:
1. Define the search space with two endpoints: a known-good state and a known-bad state. Timeline, code, process, roster — whatever mine is. State both endpoints explicitly.
2. Name the midpoint and the exact test I run there. It must return a clean binary that tells me which half to throw away.
3. Compute the number of steps to isolate the answer, and lay out the full bisection sequence: if the test passes, next test X; if it fails, next test Y.
4. Say what the search space is that this cannot bisect, if any.

Then: give me the first test to run today and what each result eliminates.

Rules:
- The failure is proposing investigations that narrow nothing. Every test must halve the space. If your first move doesn't eliminate roughly half of the candidates, it is not this model — throw it out and find one that does.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M131.use: When deciding whether to try something new or double down
M131.body:
Apply **Exploration vs. Exploitation** to my situation.

Every choice under uncertainty trades gathering information about untried options against harvesting the best option known so far. The optimal mix depends on how much time remains to use what you learn: explore when the horizon is long, exploit when it is short. Regret usually comes from exploiting too early.

Do this:
1. State my horizon in a unit: how many more decisions like this will I make, or how many years remain in which learning would still pay?
2. List what I have already tried and how much I actually know about each — number of trials, not vibes. Then name my current best-known option.
3. List the untried options and what one trial of each would cost, and what it would teach.
4. Given the horizon, prescribe a split: what fraction of my next N decisions should be exploration, and which specific untried option gets the next slot.

Then: tell me whether I am exploiting too early, and name the one thing to go try.

Rules:
- The horizon does the work. Any answer that doesn't move when I change the horizon has ignored the model. And "stay open-minded" is not exploration — name the option and the trial.

M132.use: When a number arrives more precise than it has any right to be
M132.body:
Apply **Garbage In, Garbage Out** to my situation.

No processing sophistication rescues bad inputs; a flawless model fed corrupt data produces confident nonsense, and the polish of the output disguises the rot at the source.

Do this:
1. Ignore the analysis, the model, the conclusion. Go straight to the feedstock: list every input, number, and assumption my situation rests on.
2. For each, name its origin. Who produced it, how, when, and what incentive did they have? Flag any that is itself the output of another estimate.
3. Assign each input an honest error bar or a quality grade, and say which is the worst.
4. Propagate: restate my conclusion carrying the precision the weakest input actually supports. Round it down until it is honest.

Then: tell me whether my conclusion survives its own inputs, and which single input to go verify first.

Rules:
- Do not critique the method or the reasoning — that is admiring the machinery. This model only looks upstream at the feedstock. And do not accept a number just because it was rendered to two decimals.

M133.use: When progress has plateaued and effort isn't helping
M133.body:
Apply **Greedy Algorithms and Local Optima** to my situation.

Always taking the best immediate step lands you on the nearest hilltop, which is usually not the mountain. Improvement stalls not because you are at the top but because every neighboring move looks worse. The fix is not more effort in the current direction — it is a deliberate jump that accepts a temporary loss to reach a better basin.

Do this:
1. Describe the hilltop I am standing on: the local maximum my sequence of sensible next-best steps has walked me to.
2. Test the plateau. List the adjacent moves available and show that each one looks worse in the short run. If any adjacent move looks better, I am not stuck and you should say so.
3. Name two or three distant basins — positions I cannot reach by any incremental step from here.
4. For each, price the jump: how deep is the valley, how long, and what is the peak worth if I land it.

Then: tell me whether to keep climbing or jump, and what the jump costs before it pays.

Rules:
- Do not smuggle in an incremental improvement and call it a jump. A jump must be worse before it is better — if your proposal has no valley, it isn't one.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M134.use: When pricing whether new information is worth anything
M134.body:
Apply **Information and Entropy (Shannon)** to my situation.

Information is measured by how much uncertainty a message removes. The expected message carries almost nothing; the surprising one carries a lot. A source that confirms what I already believed has low information content.

Do this:
1. State my current uncertainty as a probability. What do I already believe, and how confidently, before any new data arrives?
2. List each piece of evidence or data source available to me. For each, predict what it will say before I look.
3. Score each one on surprise: if the source can only tell me what I already predicted, its information content is near zero — mark it and discard it. Rank the rest by how much they would move my probability.
4. Name the channel: how much of the high-information stuff can actually reach me and get acted on, given the volume of low-information traffic in the way?

Then: tell me which single source to buy or read, and which to stop consuming entirely.

Rules:
- Volume is not information. The misuse is scoring sources by how much they produce or how authoritative they sound. Score them only by uncertainty removed — and cut the confirming ones even when they feel good.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M135.use: When deciding where to build in slack, spares, or backups
M135.body:
Apply **Redundancy and Error Correction** to my situation.

Reliable systems are built from unreliable parts by adding deliberate excess — duplicates, checksums, parity — so failures are detected and absorbed rather than propagated. The question is never whether parts fail, but whether one failure can take the whole thing down.

Do this:
1. Assume every component in my situation fails. List the single points of failure: the parts whose failure alone is fatal — person, supplier, account, relationship, machine.
2. For each, name the detection mechanism. How would I learn it failed, and how long after? Silent failure is the expensive kind.
3. Check correlation: which of my apparent backups fail at the same time and for the same reason as the primary? Those are not redundancy.
4. Price it. Cost of duplication versus cost of that specific failure, and rank where to buy spares.

Then: name the one uncorrelated backup worth paying for, and the fake backup I should stop counting on.

Rules:
- Do not recommend redundancy uniformly — that is just expensive. And do not count a duplicate that shares a failure mode with the original; correlated backups are the classic way this model gets faked.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M136.use: When you're checking, measuring, or reading something constantly
M136.body:
Apply **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** to my situation.

What matters is not the amount of data but the ratio of meaningful variation to random variation within it. Adding volume often adds noise faster than signal — measuring a slow-moving quantity frequently mostly increases noise, which is why checking a portfolio daily makes you worse-informed.

Do this:
1. Name the underlying quantity I actually care about, and how fast it truly changes: per day, per quarter, per year.
2. Name how often I currently look at it or receive updates about it.
3. Compare the two rates. Say what fraction of the variation I am observing is real movement versus sampling noise, and give me a number even if estimated.
4. Prescribe a new observation interval matched to the real rate of change, and list the specific inputs, feeds, and reports to stop consuming entirely.

Then: tell me how often to look, and what to stop looking at.

Rules:
- The misuse is concluding "gather more data." This model usually says the opposite: look less often, ignore most inputs. If your answer increases what I monitor, you have inverted it — justify that hard or reverse it.

M137.use: When shortcuts have made everything slower than it should be
M137.body:
Apply **Technical Debt** to my situation.

Expedient shortcuts borrow speed today against compounding future friction, and the interest is paid in every subsequent change rather than in one visible bill. It generalizes past software: deferred maintenance, undocumented processes, hacked-together org structures. Debt is legitimate only if you are tracking it and intend to repay it.

Do this:
1. Inventory the shortcuts in my situation. For each, state what was gained at the time and roughly when it was taken on.
2. Price the interest, not the principal: how much extra time, coordination, or risk does each shortcut add to every single change I now make? Give a per-change cost.
3. Multiply by my expected change rate to get the annual carry. Rank the shortcuts by carry, not by how ugly they look.
4. For each of the top items, decide explicitly: repay, refinance, or keep paying and accept it. Say which, and when repayment happens.

Then: tell me my true annual interest bill, and the one debt to retire first.

Rules:
- Do not describe the mess. The model's work is pricing the recurring interest and forcing a repay-or-accept decision. Untracked debt with no repayment date is not a trade-off, it is a default.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M138.use: When someone demands a guaranteed answer in advance
M138.body:
Apply **Undecidability and the Halting Problem** to my situation.

Some questions have no general procedure that answers them — you cannot determine in advance whether an arbitrary program finishes without running it and waiting. The transferable lesson is that for certain systems there is no shortcut to the outcome, and demanding a guaranteed advance answer is demanding something that provably does not exist.

Do this:
1. Sort the questions in my situation into two piles: those answerable by analysis in advance, and those answerable only by running the thing and observing.
2. For each item in the second pile, say what makes it undecidable in advance — the feedback, the self-reference, the dependence on how others respond to my move.
3. For each undecidable one, stop trying to predict and design the experiment instead: the cheapest run that produces the observation.
4. Attach a stopping rule to each run: how long I wait, and what I do when time expires without a verdict. That is the only halting guarantee available.

Then: tell me what I must stop trying to know in advance, and the timeout I run instead.

Rules:
- The misuse is invoking this as a license for vagueness about everything. Most questions are decidable — be strict about which are genuinely not, and demand real analysis on the rest.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M139.use: When choosing between something cheap and something excellent
M139.body:
Apply **A great business at a fair price beats a fair business at a great price** to my situation.

Statistical cheapness caps your return at the discount and consumes attention. A business with durable high returns on capital compounds for you after you buy it. Pay up for quality rather than down for junk.

Do this:
1. Classify each option in front of me: cheap-and-mediocre, or excellent-at-a-fair-price. Say what the cheap one's return actually depends on — usually just the gap closing, once.
2. For each, state the return on capital and whether it is durable. Name the specific thing that keeps it high, and what would erode it.
3. Run the clock forward. Model both over my real holding period, not the next quarter. The cheap one stops paying at the discount; the quality one keeps compounding.
4. Charge the cheap option for attention: the hours of monitoring, re-deciding, and exit-timing it demands.

Then: tell me which to own for the period I will actually hold it.

Rules:
- "Quality" is not a story about a nice brand or a good management team. It must be a durable high return on capital with a named mechanism. And do not reprice the junk as quality because it is cheap — junk stays junk.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M140.use: Before trusting a recommendation from someone paid to give it
M140.body:
Apply **Agency costs (principal-agent problem)** to my situation.

Whenever one party acts for another, the agent's interests diverge from the principal's, and the gap is paid in bad decisions, hidden fees, and self-dealing — especially where the agent is paid by someone other than the person he advises.

Do this:
1. List every agent acting on my behalf here: advisors, brokers, managers, employees, lawyers, vendors, anyone recommending anything.
2. For each, trace the money. Who actually pays them, how much, on what trigger, and what happens to their pay if I do nothing at all?
3. Name the specific decision each agent would push me toward that serves them and not me. Be concrete — the transaction, the product, the headcount, the delay.
4. Prescribe: realign the pay, change who they report to, or discount their advice by a stated amount. Say which for each.

Then: tell me whose advice to discount, and the one incentive to restructure.

Rules:
- This is not about anyone's honesty or intentions. Good people follow their incentives without noticing. If your analysis turns on whether someone is trustworthy, you have missed the model — follow the compensation.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M141.use: When a company's edge might just be current taste
M141.body:
Apply **Avoid style companies (fashion risk)** to my situation.

Businesses whose demand rests on taste or fashion have no durable claim on the customer — the thing that made them work is the thing that will leave. The competitive position is really just a current preference.

Do this:
1. Ask the only question that matters: why does the customer come back? Answer it in one sentence, from the customer's mouth, not the company's marketing.
2. Sort that reason into one of two bins. Durable: habit, addiction, switching cost, contract, network, genuine utility, distribution. Fashion: it is what people like right now.
3. If fashion, estimate the lease term. How long has this preference already run, what killed the last three things in this category, and what replaces it?
4. Test the pricing power. If the company raised prices 10 percent, does the customer pay, or does the taste simply move on?

Then: tell me whether I own a title or a lease, and how long the lease has left.

Rules:
- Current growth and current margins are exactly what a style company looks like at its peak — they are evidence of nothing. Do not let strong recent numbers stand in for durability.

M142.use: When people are behaving in ways that make no rational sense
M142.body:
Apply **Behavioral economics (psychology-informed economics)** to my situation.

The rational-man model is a useful first approximation that systematically fails where human psychology predictably deviates from it. Economics is only correct when the psychology is bolted back on.

Do this:
1. Run the rational-actor model first, explicitly. What would purely self-interested calculating agents do here? State the prediction.
2. State what is actually happening. Name the gap between the two precisely — the specific behavior the rational model cannot explain.
3. Name the psychological tendency producing the gap, and say which party is under its influence. Not a vague list of biases: identify the one doing the work, and the incentive or social pressure that switched it on.
4. Redo the prediction with the psychology bolted on, and say what I should do differently given the real behavior rather than the modeled behavior.

Then: give me a forecast of what these people will actually do, not what they should do.

Rules:
- Naming a bias is not analysis. This model earns its keep only when the psychology changes the prediction — if your revised forecast matches the rational one, you have decorated, not explained.

M143.use: When risk has been handed to you as a single number
M143.body:
Apply **Beta-as-risk / CAPM / modern portfolio theory critique** to my situation.

Volatility is not risk. Risk is the permanent loss of capital. Models that substitute price wobble for danger — beta, CAPM, MPT — are precise where they should be approximately right.

Do this:
1. Find every place risk has been quantified here by a volatility measure: beta, standard deviation, Sharpe, VaR, a risk score. Quote the number and say what it actually measures.
2. Throw it out and rebuild. List the concrete scenarios in which my capital is permanently gone and does not come back — the business dies, the thesis was wrong, the debt comes due, I am forced to sell at the bottom.
3. For each, give probability and magnitude of permanent loss. Rough numbers, labeled as estimates.
4. Compare. Say where the volatility number and the permanent-loss analysis disagree, and which of my decisions flips as a result.

Then: tell me the real risk of permanent loss, and what the volatility number was hiding.

Rules:
- Do not compute a better beta or a smarter volatility metric — that is the error, not the fix. Also: a low-volatility asset can be enormously risky and a jumpy one nearly safe. Track only permanent loss.

M144.use: When prosperity rests on claims nobody has checked yet
M144.body:
Apply **Bezzle (Galbraith)** to my situation.

Between the moment an embezzlement occurs and the moment it is discovered, two people feel rich on the same money. The inventory of undiscovered theft — or of unverified claims — is a real, spending-stimulating asset, until it isn't.

Do this:
1. Find the unchecked claims in my situation: assets marked at a price nobody tested, revenue that has not been collected, promises counted as done, valuations set by the last transaction rather than by cash.
2. For each, identify who is currently feeling rich on it. If two parties are both counting the same money or the same commitment, name both.
3. Name the checking event — the audit, the redemption, the sale, the funding round, the customer actually paying — and estimate when it arrives. The bezzle only ends when someone checks.
4. Say what the number becomes on the far side of the check, and who is holding it when the shortfall lands.

Then: tell me how much of the prosperity here is bezzle, and when it gets checked.

Rules:
- This is not an accusation of fraud. The bezzle grows through optimism and lax verification as readily as theft. Look for the unverified claim, not the villain — hunting for a crook is how this model gets misused.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M145.use: When a well-funded org fails at something simple
M145.body:
Apply **Bureaucratic dysfunction (bureaucracy as a cancer)** to my situation.

Large organizations metastasize internal process that consumes effort and diffuses responsibility, producing collective stupidity no individual in them would produce alone. It does not stop on its own; it must be cut back deliberately.

Do this:

3. For each cancerous step, say what it was originally installed to prevent, and price that harm against the effort the step now consumes.
4. Propose which steps to cut, and the one named owner who inherits the responsibility they were diffusing.

Then: give me a cut list I could execute this quarter, each item with an accountable name attached.

Rules:
- Do not answer with better process. Process is the disease — a new coordination layer, committee, or tracking tool is the cancer growing. Only removal counts.

M146.use: When a return looks great and you can't see who's paying
M146.body:
Apply **Casino take + Ponzi + bubble decomposition** to my situation.

An apparent return has up to four parts: real production, the frictional skim taken by intermediaries, the chain-letter component paying early entrants out of late entrants' money, and pure speculative froth. Only the first is wealth; the rest is transfer.

Do this:
1. Name where actual value is produced here. What cash arrives from outside the pool of participants, and from whom, for what?
2. Count the skim: every intermediary taking a cut — fees, spreads, promoters, commissions, gas, rake — as an annual percentage of capital in.
3. Trace the chain: are current returns funded by later entrants' money? Follow one dollar from a new entrant to an existing holder.
4. Whatever return is left is froth — price paid above production. Size it.

Then: split the advertised return into production / skim / chain / froth, in percentages.

Rules:
- Don't just call it a bubble. Do the arithmetic on each component separately, with numbers. And don't let "there's real technology here" excuse the skim — genuine production and a casino take coexist happily.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M147.use: Before specializing away or outsourcing a capability
M147.body:
Apply **Comparative advantage (Ricardo) — and its second-order effects** to my situation.

Both parties gain from trade when each specializes where its relative cost is lowest, even if one is absolutely better at everything. The theorem is true and static; it says nothing about who accumulates capability over the following decades.

Do this:
1. Name the two parties and the two activities. For each party, state the cost of each activity in terms of the other activity forgone — relative, never absolute.
2. Show the static gain: who specializes where, and what each side pockets.
3. Now run the decade. For each party, name what capability, learning curve, supplier base, and talent pool accumulates from what it specialized in — and what atrophies on the other side.
4. Say whether the static gain still holds once that capability drift is priced in.

Then: the trade to make, plus the specific capability I must deliberately keep even though it is not my comparative advantage.

Rules:
- Two ways to fake this: comparing absolute costs (that isn't the theorem), and stopping at step 2 because the math is clean. Munger's whole contribution was step 3.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M148.use: When projecting a position past the current decade
M148.body:
Apply **Creative / competitive destruction** to my situation.

New technologies and business models don't add to the economy, they kill incumbents. The same force that created my position will eventually come for it.

Do this:
1. Name what my position displaced in order to exist. What died so this could live — and how long did that incumbent feel safe first?
2. List the specific mechanisms of the destroyer: what it did cheaper or faster, and why the incumbent structurally could not copy it without wrecking its own model.
3. Point those same mechanisms back at me. Who is doing to my position what mine did to that one? Name candidates even if they look small, crude, or low-status today.
4. State the economic conditions my position requires to keep working, and which of them those candidates dissolve.

Then: name the decade in which my current position stops working, and the earliest observable sign it has started.

M149.use: When performance outruns what strategy and numbers explain
M149.body:
Apply **Culture as a compounding competitive force** to my situation.

A transmitted culture — trust, thrift, candor, seamless delegation — lowers an organization's real costs and error rate in ways rivals cannot buy or copy. It compounds, and it can be destroyed far faster than it was built.

Do this:
1. State the performance gap here that the strategy, the financials, and the talent roster do not account for.
2. List the specific costs a high-trust culture removes in my case: verification, approvals, contracts, legal review, monitoring, rework, hedging, politics. Estimate each in headcount or hours.
3. Name the transmission mechanism: who models the behavior, and how a new hire learns it in month one. If there is none, this isn't culture compounding — it's just the current staff, and it leaves when they do.
4. Name the three actions most likely to destroy it fast: one tolerated exception, one hire, one incentive.

Then: what my culture is worth in cost terms, and the one thing to refuse because it would burn the account faster than any deal could refill it.

- Values statements, offsites, and posters are not culture. Count only behavior that shows up as a cost my competitor pays and I don't.

M150.use: When designing who gets to decide what
M150.body:
Apply **Decentralization short of abdication** to my situation.

Pushing authority down to trustworthy operators buys speed, ownership, and almost no overhead. The price is paid up front, in ruthless selection. The failure mode is abdication — decentralizing to people who were never worth trusting.

Do this:
1. List every decision currently escalated above the operator. For each, price the error it prevents against the delay and disownership it creates.
2. Rate each operator on the only two things that make trust safe: track record, and character under pressure. Be specific, not charitable.
3. Push each decision down to the lowest level where the operator rates high on both. Decisions that can't go down are selection problems, not control problems — say so.
4. Draw the abdication line: the few things that never decentralize (capital allocation, hiring the operator, reputation-threatening risk), plus the one signal that tells me trust was misplaced before it's fatal.

Then: the specific list of what I stop deciding, and the tripwire that tells me I picked wrong.

Rules:
- Don't hand me a control apparatus, dashboard, or approval matrix — that's the thing this model refuses. And decentralizing without ruthless up-front selection isn't this model, it's abdication wearing its clothes.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M151.use: Before a move justified by breadth, balance, or synergy
M151.body:
Apply **Deworsification** to my situation.

Expansion into businesses outside your competence — usually by acquisition, usually because the other field looks more attractive from a distance — reliably makes the enterprise worse, not more diversified.

Do this:
1. State what the new thing is, and define my existing competence strictly: the domain where I know more than the person on the other side of the table.
2. List every reason given for the move. Circle any that are breadth, balance, diversification, synergy, or the appeal of a hotter market. Those are the tell, not the argument.
3. Name what I know about the new field that its incumbents don't — and ask who is selling into this, and what they know that I don't. If both answers are empty, say so.
4. Price the attention this takes off the core business, in the currency of the person whose attention it is: mine.

Then: a verdict — competence extension or deworsification — and the single fact that would flip it.

Rules:
- "It's adjacent" and "there are synergies" are exactly what deworsification always says about itself. Don't grade the new business on its own attractiveness. Grade my ability to run it against the people already there.

M152.use: When work crawls and everyone does a bit of everything
M152.body:
Apply **Division of labor / specialization (Adam Smith's pin factory)** to my situation.

Splitting a task among specialists multiplies output far past what the same people produce working end-to-end. Smith's pin factory was a designed system, not a spontaneous one — the design is the whole trick.

Do this:
1. Break the work I described into its actual sequence of distinct operations, pin-factory style — as many as it really has, not the tidy three.
2. For each operation, name the skill it requires and the setup or context-switch cost paid every time a person moves into or out of it.
3. Reassign: give each operation to whoever can repeat it, and design the handoff explicitly — what passes, in what state, on what trigger. The handoff is where the gains leak.
4. Name the operations that must stay whole because splitting them destroys the judgment.

Then: a specific division of the work, and the throughput multiple I should expect from it.

Rules:
- Silos and job titles are not division of labor. Count only splits that let someone repeat one operation until they're fast at it, with a designed handoff. And name the cost: where specialists stop being able to see the whole pin.

M153.use: When you're asked to trust a number you can't check
M153.body:
Apply **Double-entry bookkeeping as a civilizational technology** to my situation.

Double entry is an error-checking convention that lets strangers trust an enterprise's numbers — a lie has to be told twice and still balance. That checkability is what makes commerce at scale possible. Degrade the convention and you degrade everything running on it.

Do this:
1. Name the number strangers are being asked to trust here, and the entity whose word currently backs it.
2. Find the other side of the entry. Every claimed asset or gain must correspond to somebody's liability, some cash movement, some named counterparty. Trace it. If nothing balances it, say what is actually being counted.
3. Name what does the error-checking here — audit, reconciliation, escrow, third-party confirmation — and say who would catch a false entry, and how fast.
4. State what a permissive version of that convention would let through, and who eats the loss when trust is withdrawn.

Then: a verdict on whether this number is checkable or merely asserted, and the one check that would make it trustworthy to a stranger.

Rules:
- This is not "keep good records." The model is about the checking constraint, not the tidiness. If there's no stranger and no counterparty, the model doesn't apply.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M154.use: When the headline profit figure is a non-GAAP construct
M154.body:
Apply **EBITDA and adjusted earnings as corrupted accounting** to my situation.

Metrics that exclude real costs — depreciation, stock compensation, "one-time" charges — are not earnings, they are advocacy. Accounting is the language of business, and a corrupted dialect corrupts the thinking of everyone who speaks it.

Do this:
1. Take the headline number and list every item excluded from it, one line each, with dollars.
2. For each exclusion, answer one question: did cash leave, or will it have to? Depreciation means the machines wear out and get bought again. Stock comp means shareholders were diluted. A "one-time" charge that appears annually is an operating cost.
3. Rebuild the number with every real cost put back in. State what's left.
4. Name who chose this metric and what their compensation is tied to.

Then: the actual owner earnings, and the size of the gap between that and what I was told.

Rules:
- The fake version is accepting an adjustment because "everyone in this industry reports it that way." Everyone speaking a corrupted dialect is the disease, not the excuse. Substitute "bullshit earnings" for EBITDA wherever it appears and see whether the argument survives.

M155.use: When a forecast claims physics-grade precision
M155.body:
Apply **Economics as a complex adaptive system (not physics)** to my situation.

Economies are adaptive systems whose participants react to the models made about them. They have no invariant constants, so precision here is usually false precision dressed in physics envy.

Do this:
1. Quote the model, forecast, or number in my situation that is claiming rigor — including its decimal places, confidence intervals, and projection horizon.
2. List the constants it silently assumes hold: elasticity, correlation, conversion rate, churn, multiple, beta. For each, name a plausible reason it has already changed since it was measured.
3. Find the reflexive loop: who acts differently *because* this model exists and is believed, and what does their reaction do to the model's own inputs?
4. Restate the claim honestly — as a range, a direction, or "unknowable" — and say which decision changes if it's wrong by 2x.

Then: which parts of this analysis survive as a direction, and which digits are false precision I should stop quoting out loud.

Rules:
- This is not a license to ignore numbers or shrug that it's complicated. The model demands you name the specific constant that isn't constant and the specific feedback loop that eats the prediction. Vague uncertainty-mongering is the fake version.

M156.use: When asking whether an incumbent's size is a real moat
M156.body:
Apply **Economies of scale / advantages of scale** to my situation.

Size confers real advantages — fixed costs spread over more units, better information, distribution and shelf presence, and the psychological one where ubiquity itself reads as social proof. In many industries these are self-reinforcing.

Do this:
1. Split the cost structure into fixed and variable. Show what per-unit cost does when volume doubles — actual numbers, not the assertion.
2. Go through the non-cost advantages separately and score each present or absent here: shelf/attention share, information advantage from more data, ability to advertise where small rivals can't, ubiquity as social proof.
3. Find the diseconomies: at what size do bureaucracy, complexity, and lost focus start eating the gains, and by what mechanism?
4. State whether scale here is self-reinforcing (winner takes most) or flat past a threshold — and where the threshold sits.

Then: whether size is a real moat in my case or just a bigger version of the same thing, and the volume at which it starts to matter.

Rules:
- The fake version asserts scale advantages that never touch the cost line or the customer. If doubling doesn't lower per-unit cost, buy better information, or make me more visible, there is no advantage of scale — only more of me.

M157.use: When you must be structurally, not incrementally, better
M157.body:
Apply **Extreme maximization or minimization of one or two variables** to my situation.

Some positions are won by taking a single variable to a ludicrous extreme — Costco's relentless minimization of markup — creating a position rivals cannot match without destroying their own model. The extremity is the moat.

Do this:
1. List the variables that matter to whoever I serve. Pick the one or two where an absurd extreme would be both visible and valued.
2. State the extreme as a number at a level that sounds unreasonable. Not 20% better — the floor or the ceiling.
3. The crux: for each major rival, say exactly what they'd have to give up to match it — which revenue line, which channel, which customer they'd have to fire. If any could match without pain, the variable is wrong. Go back to step 1.
4. Design what makes the extreme sustainable rather than a subsidy: the structural thing that lowers my cost of being extreme.

Then: the one variable to push past what rivals can follow, and the specific self-harm each must accept to follow it.

Rules:
- The fake version is "we'll be the best at everything," or an extreme nobody notices. One or two variables only, and the test isn't that it's better — it's that matching it costs a rival something they cannot afford to give.

M158.use: When an excellent competitor has no obvious secret
M158.body:
Apply **Extreme of good performance over many factors** to my situation.

The other winning template: no single dramatic variable, but many small advantages executed relentlessly — Toyota, Les Schwab — whose combination is nearly impossible to copy because there is nothing single to copy.

Do this:

4. Name what holds the pile together — the system or culture that keeps fifteen small things done right without anyone policing them.

M159.use: When pressure to act is being mistaken for a reason to
M159.body:
Apply **Fat pitch discipline (waiting for no-brainers)** to my situation.

There is no penalty for not swinging. Wait — possibly years — until an opportunity is so obviously good it needs no elaborate argument, then act decisively and with size.

Do this:
1. State the case for this opportunity in two sentences a smart twelve-year-old would nod at. If it needs a model, a deck, or three chained assumptions, you have your answer.

3. Write down what an actual fat pitch in my domain looks like — the concrete conditions and the price — as a standing rule I'd recognize on sight.
4. State what waiting literally costs me per month, in dollars or time.

Then: swing or don't, plus the written condition that would make me swing hard.

M160.use: When everyone feels rich on paper
M160.body:
Apply **Febezzle / febezzlement** to my situation.

A febezzle is the functional equivalent of embezzlement with no embezzler: wealth someone feels and spends that isn't there. Because nothing illegal happens, it grows lawfully to enormous size, and both sides feel rich until it unwinds.

Do this:
1. Find the wealth being felt. What number is making someone here — me, my staff, my investors, my customers — behave as though they are richer than they were?
2. Trace it to cash from an outside party. If it instead comes from a mark-to-market, a fresh valuation, an unexpensed cost, a paper gain, or spending booked as an asset, flag it as febezzle.
3. Quantify the real cash flowing out on the strength of that paper: compensation, hiring, capex, lifestyle, cross-investment, commitments.
4. Describe the unwind: what makes the number vanish, who is holding the spending commitments when it does, and how fast the feeling reverses.

Then: the size of the febezzle here, and the real cash exposure riding on it.

Rules:
- The fake version is calling this fraud and hunting villains. Nobody stole anything — that is the entire point, and it's why it grows so large. Look for lawful, accepted, cheerful accounting.

M161.use: When asking how a business funds its own growth
M161.body:
Apply **Float / negative working capital as automatic leverage** to my situation.

Businesses that collect cash before they pay it out — insurance premiums, subscriptions, deposits, supplier terms — are financed by their counterparties: leverage with no lender, no covenants, no margin calls, sometimes at negative cost.

Do this:
1. Map the cash cycle: the date money arrives and the date it leaves, for each major flow. Compute the gap in days.
2. Multiply that gap by daily volume to size the float — the money I hold that isn't mine. State it in dollars.
3. Price it. Am I paying for the float (a discount given, a worse price accepted, a service obligation assumed) or being paid to hold it? Compare against what a bank would charge for the same balance.
4. Test its stability: what makes it shrink fast — a growth stall, a terms change, a refund wave, churn, a run? Float that vanishes when growth stops is not capital.

Then: how much of my growth is currently financed by counterparties, and what the balance sheet looks like the day the float stops growing.

Rules:
- The fake version celebrates negative working capital while ignoring that it reverses violently. Float is leverage only if it is stable and cheap — price the run, not just the gap.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.

M162.use: When "it creates jobs" is the defense for the spending
M162.body:
Apply **Foolexures** to my situation.

A foolexure is a foolish expenditure defended on the grounds that it stimulates the economy — waste dressed up as macroeconomic virtue. Digging holes and filling them in employs people too.

Do this:
1. Find every expenditure here justified by its effect on someone other than the buyer: it creates jobs, it supports the ecosystem, it keeps the team busy, it's an investment in the community, it's good for the industry.
2. For each, ask the only question that matters: does the buyer receive something worth the price? Answer yes or no, and name the thing received.
3. For every "no," state what the same money would have bought if spent on something the buyer actually wanted. That difference is the real cost, and it is borne by someone specific — name them.
4. Separate honest gifts from foolexures. A gift known to be a gift is fine. A waste calling itself a stimulus is not.

Then: the list of spending to cut, and the exact virtue-language each one is hiding behind.

M163.use: Before any plan that assumes rivals stand still
M163.body:
Apply **Game theory** to my situation.

Outcomes are the equilibrium of interacting strategies, not the execution of one actor's plan. My rival's best response is part of my own payoff.

Do this:
1. Name every player with a move here — including the ones I've been treating as scenery: regulators, suppliers, my own employees, the other side's board, the incumbent I ignored.
2. For each, state their actual payoff — what that human is personally optimizing, which is often not what their organization wants.
3. Take my plan and write each player's best response to it. Then my response to that. Then theirs. Go three moves deep, minimum.
4. State where it settles. Then ask the design question: what commitment, contract, price, or irreversible move changes their payoffs so the equilibrium lands somewhere I like?

Then: the likely equilibrium of my current plan, and the one change to the game — not to my play — that makes a better equilibrium stable.

- The fake version names a prisoner's dilemma and stops, or quietly assumes rivals stand still while I execute. Every prediction here must survive the sentence "and then they respond." Flag any part of my plan that only works if the other side plays badly.

M164.use: When the worst actor in your market sets the terms
M164.body:
Apply **Gresham's law (bad drives out good)** to my situation.

Bad money drives out good — and in Munger's extension, so do bad lending, bad accounting, and bad conduct, because the reckless competitor sets the terms everyone else must match to survive.

Do this:
1. Name the worst actor in my market or organization: whoever is willing to do the thing I am not.
2. State exactly what they do and what it buys them — lower price, faster close, looser terms, a better-looking number, a shorter approval.
3. Trace the coercion chain: who has to match them to stay alive, then who has to match those, and how many steps until their standard is the standard. Say where I currently sit in that chain.
4. List the only real exits — a rule binding everyone (regulation, an enforced norm, a standard), a customer segment that pays for the good version, or leaving the market. Say which is actually available to me.

Then: whether my standards are still mine or already set by the worst actor, and which exit is real.

Rules:
- The fake version is moral commentary. This law is mechanical: good is driven out because bad is cheaper and legal, not because people are wicked. Note the asymmetry — my refusing to match doesn't stop it, it only removes me.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M165.use: Before betting that the consensus price is wrong
M165.body:
Apply **Hard-form efficient market theory (named and rejected)** to my situation.

The claim that prices always fully reflect all information is a real insight pushed to a false extreme by physics envy. The usable version: efficiency is the default, not a law. Markets are mostly efficient — which is not always.

Do this:
1. Start from the presumption that I'm wrong. State the consensus price, estimate, or belief here, and make the strongest case that it is already correct.
2. Name who set it: how many of them, how well paid, how well informed, how recently. That is the efficiency of this particular market, measured.
3. Find the structural reason efficiency fails *here* — forced sellers, a mandate that excludes buyers, too small to be worth anyone's time, a taboo, information only I hold, a horizon nobody else can sit through. "I did more work" and "I have a view" are not on this list.
4. If step 3 comes up empty, conclude the price is right and I have no edge.

Then: either the named inefficiency I'm exploiting, or an honest verdict that I'd be paying to discover the market was efficient.

Rules:
- Two fakes, opposite directions: treating efficiency as a law and never looking, or assuming an edge because I have an opinion. The burden of proof sits on inefficiency, and it must be a reason others *can't* act — not merely haven't.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
- If this model genuinely doesn't fit my situation, say so plainly and stop. Don't force it.
- Work only from what I have actually told you. If a step needs a fact I haven't given you — a number, a precedent, a name, a date — write UNRESOLVED and tell me what to go find. M166.use: When a projection's only defense is that it hasn't broken
M166.body:
Apply **Herb Stein's law** to my situation.

If something cannot go on forever, it will stop. A trend that requires impossible arithmetic to continue does not need a trigger to end — only time.

Do this:
1. Name the trend and extend it mechanically at its current rate: 5, 10, 25 years. Do the arithmetic and print the absurd number that falls out.
2. Say what physical or accounting limit that number violates — total market size, the population, the customer's entire income, available land, hours in a day, GDP.
3. Compute when the trend reaches roughly 10% of that limit. That is approximately when it must decelerate, no matter how healthy it looks today.
4. Describe the two ways it can stop — gradual deceleration or abrupt break — say which the structure here favors, and what I would observe first.

Then: the date range by which this trend has to stop, and what my plan looks like if it stops at the early end of that range.

M167.use: Before paying anyone to beat the boring default
M167.body:
Apply **Indexing** to my situation.

After costs, active management is a negative-sum game — the crowd cannot beat the crowd. The cheap, whole-market default is the honest baseline any active approach must beat, not merely match.

Do this:
1. Name the index for my situation: the cheap, do-nothing-clever default. In investing it's the total market; elsewhere it's the standard tool, the boring incumbent, the off-the-shelf process. State its expected return and its cost.
2. Total what the active alternative costs relative to that: fees, my own time priced honestly, the errors, taxes and switching, the option value burned.
3. State the hurdle — index return plus everything in step 2. That is the number active must clear just to break even.
4. Name my specific, durable reason for clearing it: something I have that the average participant does not. "I'll work harder" and "I'll be careful" are not reasons.

Then: index or go active, with the hurdle number I'd have to beat every year — not once.

Rules:
- The fake version compares active's gross result to the index and declares victory. Costs and my own time are the entire point, and matching the index while paying for the attempt is a loss, not a tie.

M168.use: When self-interest is argued to produce a good outcome
M168.body:
Apply **Invisible foot (Hardin)** to my situation.

The malign twin of the invisible hand: the same self-interested behavior, under different rules, produces harm nobody intended — pollution, commons collapse, arms races. The difference is only whether the costs fall inside the transaction.

Do this:
1. State the transaction: who buys, who sells, what each gets. Then draw the boundary and name who is *not* at the table.
2. For each party outside that boundary, name the specific cost they bear: pollution, congestion, attention, a depleted commons, an arms race they must now join, a risk quietly socialized.
3. Multiply it out. If every rational actor here does this, what does the shared resource look like in ten years? Name the thing that gets exhausted, and by when.
4. Ask the design question: what rule, price, or property boundary would pull the external cost back inside the transaction — and who has the power to enforce it?

Then: whether the invisible-hand argument here survives once the outsiders' costs are counted, and the specific rule change that would make this self-interest benign again.

Rules:
- The fake version labels any harm an externality. The test is narrow: produced by rational self-interest under current rules, borne by someone outside the deal, and worse when everyone does it. If a villain is required, this is the wrong model.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.

M169.use: When deciding whether to design coordination or let it emerge
M169.body:
Apply **Invisible hand (Adam Smith)** to my situation.

Self-interested actors trading under real prices produce coordination nobody intended or designed. The mechanism is price signals carrying information between people who never meet — and it only runs when its preconditions hold.

Do this:
1. List every actor in my situation and name what each is selfishly optimizing for. Not what they say — what they'd do.
2. Identify the signal that carries information between them (price, demand, queue length, attrition, complaints). If there isn't one, say so.
3. Name the coordination that emerges without anyone directing it, and the coordination that only exists because someone is directing it.
4. Test the preconditions one by one: can actors freely enter and exit; are prices real and visible; does each actor bear the costs of their own choices? Mark each pass/fail.

Then: tell me which parts of my situation I should stop designing and let the actors sort out, and which parts have a broken precondition and will never self-organize.

Rules:
- Don't treat this as ideology. "Markets work" is not the model — the model is a mechanism with preconditions, and where a precondition fails the hand simply isn't there.

M170.use: When tracing how far one increment of spending actually ripples
M170.body:
Apply **Keynesian multiplier** to my situation.

One party's spending is another's income, so an increment of expenditure ripples outward at some multiple of itself — until leakage stops it, and it runs in reverse when the wealth funding round one turns out to be perceived rather than real.

Do this:
1. Name the initial increment in my situation and its size — the first dollar, hire, or unit of activity injected.
2. Trace three rounds concretely: who receives it, what fraction they re-spend, and where the rest leaks (saving, imports, debt repayment, fees, hoarding). Name each leak.
3. From those fractions, compute the implied multiplier and the total activity my increment supports.
4. Now run it backwards. Ask what wealth is funding round one, whether any of it is perceived rather than realized, and compute the contraction if that perception reverses.

Then: give me the true size of what my increment supports, and how much of it evaporates if the belief underneath it changes.

Rules:
- The multiplier is not magic. Every round leaks, and a prompt that skips the leakage fractions has faked the model. Never run it only upward.
- Be concrete about my situation. No generic advice I could have read anywhere.
